title: Kefta tagine
subtitle: Moroccan spiced meatballs baked in tomato sauce with eggs
excerpt: Spiced lamb and beef meatballs simmered in a cumin and paprika tomato sauce, then eggs broken over the top and cooked through in the sauce. The eggs are the finishing touch: they set into the sauce so you scoop meatball, egg, and sauce together with bread. A quick Moroccan weeknight dish.
sourceNotes: Kefta tagine is one of the everyday dishes of Moroccan cooking — found in homes and small restaurants across the country. The combination of spiced minced meat with eggs is common across North Africa and the Middle East; the Moroccan version is distinguished by cumin, paprika, and fresh herbs. It is a fast dish by tagine standards, often cooked in under 30 minutes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meatballs can be shaped and refrigerated overnight.

content.text: Kefta are Moroccan spiced meatballs, typically made from minced lamb, beef, or a mixture of both. Shape them small (about 2 cm in diameter) so they cook through quickly in the sauce. The mix of lamb and beef gives the best balance of flavour and fat; all-beef is drier.

The eggs go in at the end. Make small wells in the sauce, crack in the eggs, and cover the pan. They take 4 to 5 minutes to set to a runny yolk. Serve immediately once the eggs are set — they continue to cook in the residual heat.

items.prepNote: or half lamb mince, half beef

content.text: Combine the {{beef-mince}}, grated {{onion}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{coriander}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Mix well and shape into small balls about 2 cm across. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide, shallow pan over medium heat. Add the chopped {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{paprika-sweet}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and 100 ml of water. Simmer for 5 minutes. Add the kefta to the sauce in a single layer. Cover and cook for 12 to 15 minutes, turning once. The meatballs should be cooked through.

Make 4 small wells between the kefta. Break an {{eggs}} into each well. Cover the pan and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until the egg whites are set but the yolks are still runny. Serve immediately, straight from the pan, with khobz or flatbread.

items.symptom: Meatballs falling apart in the sauce
items.cause: Mix not worked enough or too lean
items.fix: Mix the kefta mixture vigorously by hand for 1 to 2 minutes to develop the protein. The mixture should feel slightly sticky. Some fat content is essential — pure lean mince will be crumbly

items.cause: Covered too long after adding eggs
items.fix: Check after 4 minutes. The eggs continue to cook once the pan is removed from the heat — err on the side of slightly underdone if eating immediately
Simmer the sauce for an extra 5 minutes before adding the kefta to reduce it slightly

content.text: Kefta tagine is the weeknight dish of Moroccan cooking: quick, satisfying, and made with pantry staples. In Morocco it is cooked in a glazed earthenware tagine pot over a small gas burner or charcoal brazier; a wide frying pan produces the same result. The dish is one of the most commonly cooked in Moroccan households and one of the first dishes taught to visitors who want to learn Moroccan cooking. In Britain it has become popular as a quick weeknight supper alongside couscous or crusty bread.
